findings.message: D3 treats Codex's `.git` read-only sandbox as enough to drop post-run scope-violation validation, but that protection only prevents staging/commit/branch writes. The same plan gives Codex shell-level file editing over the workspace and only prompt-injects the scope list, then has the trusted orchestrator commit afterward; out-of-scope working-tree edits could therefore still be produced and later recorded. The ADR should not remove scope-diff validation on the basis of `.git` protection alone.

D3 relies on a launch-time smoke-test as the replacement for post-run validation, but the specified checks only assert that `workspace-write` is requested and that the CLI version is in an allowed range. They do not actually verify the runtime invariant that `.git` is read-only, while later passages both admit the guarantee can break if the smoke-test misses a behavior change and say the D3 smoke-test will detect that condition. This leaves the safety gate internally inconsistent: either the smoke-test must include an actual `.git` write-failure probe, or the ADR should stop claiming D3 detects loss of the sandbox guarantee.

D3 claims scope-boundary compliance is guaranteed by the briefing contract while making post-run scope-diff validation optional, but the same section acknowledges that Codex can edit any file in the workspace. A prompt-only contract plus a possible pre-commit review does not enforce that out-of-scope working-tree edits cannot be recorded by the orchestrator, so the plan either needs a mandatory scope check before commit or must stop treating scope containment as guaranteed.

D3 claims Codex workspace-write `.git` read-only protection structurally prevents `push` along with stage/commit, but push is a remote write and is not covered by the cited empirical result, which only demonstrates local `.git` mutations such as `git add` failing. Credential isolation is therefore not merely defense-in-depth for push; it is part of the required guarantee. The plan should separate local git mutation prevention from publish prevention so implementers do not rely on `.git` read-only as a push guard.

The plan gives conflicting switch semantics for Codex: GO-01/IN-01 advertises a one-line/provider-only switch to `provider=codex`, but T001 says selecting Codex also requires `model=gpt-5.5`. If the implementation follows the provider-only contract, Codex can inherit the Claude model; if it follows T001, the advertised switch contract is false. Specify whether the model is provider-derived/defaulted or must be edited by the user.

AC-05 only checks that `GITHUB_TOKEN / SSH 鍵` are not passed as environment variables, but SSH credentials commonly flow through `SSH_AUTH_SOCK`, `HOME`/`~/.ssh`, or key files rather than an `SSH key` env var. This criterion can pass while the fixer still has push credentials, contradicting CN-02/D3's credential isolation guarantee.

The version smoke-test still accepts Codex v0.100.x through v0.114.x, but this repo's safety model says the `.git` read-only protection is only guaranteed from v0.115.0 upward. Letting older versions through means the fixer can run without the protection this wrapper is supposed to enforce.

The credential-isolation allowlist keeps `HOME`, and the comments explicitly note that `~/.ssh` remains readable. That leaves host SSH keys and other home-directory auth material available to the fixer, so the wrapper does not actually remove filesystem-based push capability as required.

`codex exec` runs inside a `set -euo pipefail` pipeline, so any non-zero Codex exit or missing `REVIEW_FIX_STATUS` sentinel aborts the script before `EXIT_CODE` is captured and before the fallback `failed` status can be emitted. That breaks the required safe mapping of launch failures to a structured wrapper result.

Credential isolation is still incomplete: the allowlist keeps `HOME`, and the wrapper only sets `GIT_SSH_COMMAND=/bin/false`. That leaves `~/.ssh` and direct SSH-based auth available to the fixer, so the promised removal of filesystem SSH-key push capability is not actually enforced.

`STATUS=$(grep ...)` runs under `set -euo pipefail`; if Codex output does not contain the sentinel, `grep` returns 1 and the shell exits before the fallback `failed` branch can run. Missing or malformed status output therefore does not fail closed as intended.

The status parser matches `REVIEW_FIX_STATUS:` anywhere in Codex output (`grep -oE ... | tail -1`) instead of requiring the final line to be the sentinel. If Codex quotes one of the status strings in its explanation or prints any extra text after it, the wrapper can misclassify the run as `completed` or `blocked_cross_scope` even though the contract was violated.

`--scope-files` is treated as optional, and the modification-boundary section is omitted entirely when the value is empty. That lets the fixer launch without an explicit file list if the caller forgets the argument or passes an empty list, which is a fail-open path for cross-scope edits.

The `env -i` isolation here also strips Codex's own auth/config home (`CODEX_HOME` / `~/.codex`). The current `codex exec --help` contract says auth still uses `CODEX_HOME`, so the spawned fixer can lose its login state and fail to start even though the goal is only to remove GitHub/SSH credentials. Preserve the Codex auth home while excluding the push credentials.

The new `track-local-review-fix-codex` wrapper has no automated coverage, so regressions in its security-critical guards (the `0.115.0` version floor, forbidden-sandbox rejection, credential isolation, and sentinel parsing) would slip through unnoticed. This is a P1 test gap because this task now gates the Codex review-fix path.

Inference: this launch path likely never delivers the briefing text to Codex. Existing in-repo Codex launchers pass the prompt as the final argv item and keep stdin null, but this wrapper redirects the briefing file on stdin and passes `-` as the positional argument. Unless `codex exec` has undocumented stdin support, the fixer will receive `-` instead of the orchestrator briefing and scope list, so the task will not run as intended.

The wrapper always exits `0` after printing `completed`, `blocked_cross_scope`, or `failed`. That masks non-completed outcomes from Cargo Make and any shell automation that relies on process status, so a failed fixer run can be reported as a successful task at the exit-code level.

`env -i` combined with `HOME=$SAFE_HOME` strips the Codex CLI's own persisted login state and any env-based API credential before `codex exec` starts. In the normal CLI-login or `CODEX_API_KEY` setup, the fixer will launch unauthenticated and fail before it can do any work.

`track-local-review-fix-codex` is declared with `script_runner = "@shell"`, which cargo-make runs through the platform shell; on this repo's Linux environment `/bin/sh` is `dash`. The new script uses bash-only syntax (`set -o pipefail`, arrays, `${!VAR}`), so the wrapper can fail before any smoke test or Codex launch runs. This makes the new fixer task unusable unless the task explicitly uses bash or is rewritten as POSIX shell.

The intended unparsable-version smoke-test branch is unreachable for a no-match version string. With `set -euo pipefail`, `CODEX_VERSION=$(codex --version 2>&1 | grep -oE ... | head -1)` exits the script immediately when `grep` finds no `x.y.z`, before the `[ -z "$CODEX_VERSION" ]` check can print the smoke-test error and return exit 2. That makes this security preflight fail with an unstructured exit instead of the documented smoke-test failure path.

The new `review-fix-lead` config leaves `model` set to `claude-opus-4-7`, but the new Codex dispatch path forwards that same field into `codex exec --model`. As a result, opting into `provider: codex` still inherits the Claude model unless the user also changes a second setting, so the documented provider-only switch is not actually usable.
